executiveSummary: The 'Alphabet Signs' Google Ads account is experiencing significant budget leakage, primarily driven by PMax campaigns that are broadly matching to highly irrelevant and low-intent search queries. Key areas of leakage include extremely generic alphabet-related searches (e.g., 'a', 'b', 'abc signs'), competitor brand terms, and queries for products or accessories outside of Alphabet Signs' core offerings (e.g., 'boat numbers', 'felt letter boards', 'marquee letter storage box'). These non-converting terms are collectively consuming an estimated $477.21/mo in ad spend, which could be reallocated to higher-performing, commercially-driven searches for high-end dimensional letters, marquee letters, church sign letters, and commercial vehicle graphics.

recommendations.title: Block Generic 'ABC' & Alphabet Searches
recommendations.desc: PMax campaigns are broadly interpreting 'alphabet' and 'abc' keywords, leading to wasted ad spend on extremely generic and non-commercial search terms. These queries (e.g., 'a', 'b', 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz', 'abc signs', 'alphabet letters com') indicate no commercial intent for high-end sign letters and are a clear drain on the budget.

recommendations.title: Exclude Irrelevant Product Types & Low-Intent Searches
recommendations.desc: Budget is being wasted on searches for products not sold by Alphabet Signs (e.g., 'boat numbers', residential 'address numbers') or low-value, non-commercial items (e.g., 'felt letter board', 'marquee letter storage box', general 'bulletin boards'). These queries do not align with the sale of high-end dimensional letters, marquee letters, church sign letters, or commercial vehicle graphics.

recommendations.title: Block Specific Competitor Brands
recommendations.desc: Queries for specific competitor brand names like 'signoutfitters', 'national readerboard supply', 'ez signs online', and 'alpha wolf decals llc' are consuming budget without conversions. Unless a specific competitor bidding strategy is in place, these terms represent leakage, diverting potential customers to competitor offerings.
